title: PROCOMER — Costa Rica's Export & Investment Promotion Model
organization: PROCOMER (Promotora del Comercio Exterior de Costa Rica)
body_markdown:
### Context

PROCOMER is Costa Rica's semi-autonomous export and investment promotion agency, 30+ years old, ~350 staff, 45 international offices. It is funded by zona franca canon plus a small export tax — not the general state budget — and reports through the Ministry of Foreign Trade.

### Key Findings

- Six-time ranked **#1 export-promotion agency in the world**; ISO-certified, KPI-driven
- Manages **800+ buyer-supplier matches**, ~3,000 local suppliers, ~1,500 exporting companies across 4,000+ products into 120 markets
- Operates **"Acá Global"** — a credit-bridge program with 50 private funds in network. Routes startups through traditional banking → Sistema Banca de Desarrollo (convertible notes up to **$500K**) → external private funds
- Costa Rica hosts **~700 multinationals** (J&J, Boston Scientific, Amazon, Intel) clustered in Alajuela, Grecia, and Cartago — "a 20-kilometer radius" enabling fast supplier coinnovation
- Opened a **Silicon Valley OPI office in February 2026** (director: Carolina Sánchez) to attract startups to Costa Rica and recruit foreign capital

### Regulatory Observations

- Costa Rican banking is structured for **consumer banking, not enterprise**. Founders typically can't access formal credit until ~8 years post-formation
- Banking regulation does **not differentiate by ticket size** — same compliance burden applies to a $100K loan as to a $5M loan, blocking smaller companies and R&D-heavy projects
- **PROCOMER cannot directly invest by law** — it can only issue non-reimbursable grants up to $40K and pay for technical assistance, training, and certifications
- No "startup law" or differentiated regulatory regime exists in Costa Rica today

### Recommendations & Insights

- **Cross-administration policy continuity** is the single largest reason PROCOMER outperforms peer agencies. Foreign-trade strategy survives 4-year political cycles; new governments ask "what are you doing and how do I support it?" rather than reset direction
- **Public-private dual perspective**: PROCOMER acts as both regulator (zona franca governance, ventanilla única integrating 80+ institutions) and market-facing partner — letting it translate private-sector pain points into policy
- **Ireland is the relevant peer**, not Singapore: similar small-country trajectory, but Ireland invested heavily in R&D in the early 2000s — Costa Rica is now attempting that pivot
- **Korea model for human capital**: pair science/engineering credentials with MBA training to bridge technical-to-commercial gap. Costa Rica has strong engineers but weak commercialization skills

### Notable Quotes

> "La banca en Costa Rica está hecha para banca consumo, no para banca empresarial."

> "Si usted no es barato, lo que le queda es generar valor — y se genera valor en investigación y desarrollo."

> "Hay una visión a largo plazo que traspasa cambios de gobierno."

title: Ruta N Medellín — Building a City-Level VC Ecosystem
body_markdown:
### Context

15 years working in Medellín's innovation ecosystem (founder, angel, corporate innovation, Georgia Tech program); ~2 years at Ruta N. **Medellín Venture Capital** is a city-level strategy launched late 2024 in partnership with the alcaldía and Empresas Públicas de Medellín. Total committed public capital: ~$50M, target $90–100M.

### Key Findings

- In 2024, Medellín startups raised only **$50–60M total** — described as "a very bad year"
- Critical insight: **97% of capital raised by Medellín startups in 2024 came from outside Medellín; only 3% local**. If exits happen, the capital recycles abroad — the Silicon Valley flywheel does not form
- Three-pronged program structure:
  1. **Formation**: 300+ angels, family-office members, and second/third-generation heirs trained since late 2024; partnerships with Babson College for VC certification
  2. **Community**: WhatsApp groups, monthly events, the "Smart Capital" event being relaunched in October. Spawned **Mei Capital**, a ~20-member angel syndicate, after participants found their preferred ticket sizes too small individually
  3. **Fund of Funds**: $90–100M target, modeled on Israel's Yozma, Mexico's Fondo de Fondos, Peru's COFIDE, and Chile's CORFO. Invests in regional micro-VCs that include Medellín startups in their portfolios
- Already deployed: $1.7M into **Bancoldex** (Colombia's national fund of funds), unlocking **$13M** for a microVC compartment; first investment $3.2M into Sima Fintech Plus, which is closing in on $10M to deploy across 30–35 fintech startups
- Built two open-data tools: **Inversómetro** (5-year VC tracking for Medellín / Colombia, identifying 15 exits in 5 years vs. the public perception of "none") and **Startia** (open-source ecosystem CRM)

### Regulatory Observations

- **Pension regulation is the unlock**: Mexico's Fondo de Fondos is the only LatAm fund-of-funds that successfully captured pension capital — and only after a regulatory change permitting pension investment in this asset class
- Family-office regulation around alternative-asset allocation could be a parallel lever

### Recommendations & Insights

- **Money follows money.** Foreign LPs will not invest in a country whose own residents won't. Local capital deployment is a precondition, not a consequence
- **Public sector should not invest directly** in startups. Catalyze via fund-of-funds and capacity building. Direct grants did not produce sustainable companies in Medellín's earlier era — the new bar is companies that "facturen, crezcan, y resuelvan un problema que valga la pena resolver"
- **Ecosystem narrative matters**: Medellín's perceived "no exits" reality, when researched, revealed 15 exits in 5 years. Surfacing this changes founder confidence
- **Open invitation for CAF to co-invest** in the Medellín VC fund of funds (CAF appeared as "potential" on the program's slide)

### Notable Quotes

> "Si los colombianos no invierten en Colombia, ¿cómo pretenden los americanos o europeos inviertan en ustedes?"

> "Las startups no son tuyas, son del emprendedor y de la ciudad, del ecosistema y del mundo."

> "Tenemos unos inversionistas muy conservadores que solo invierten en ladrillos, en ganado, en comercio."

title: Petra Secondaries — Unlocking LatAm VC Liquidity
body_markdown:
### Context

7–8 years in VC at CDC Banco de la Vivienda (Colombia) and Wayra/Telefónica (regional, ex-Brazil), with hands-on M&A, buybacks, and partial-exit experience. **Petra** is a 6-week-old LatAm secondary brokerage. Co-founders: a Dentons partner and a New York investment banker.

### Key Findings

- **8 deals in pipeline, 0 closed yet**; ~200 inbound seller offers in the first 6 weeks — supply far exceeds active matched demand
- Working transaction sizes: **$1–5M secondary positions**
- Cannot serve large positions. Cometa wants to sell $30M of Bitso — but the entire largest Colombian fund is $30M total. There is no domestic buyer pool at this scale
- Capital-flow data: **~70% of capital entering LatAm is foreign; only ~30% is local**. LatAm fund fundraising barely reaches $1B/year vs. ~$4B inbound — "the buyer money is abroad"
- Strategic next step: launching a **$500M closed-end public-listed vehicle** holding ~10 LatAm portfolio companies — modeled on Bill Ackman's Pershing Square emerging-markets approach. Target: scale to $5–10B AUM in a few years
- Fundraising to build the vehicle: **$3M for ~12 months** of regulatory, legal, banking, market-maker, and analyst infrastructure

### Regulatory Observations

- The LatAm secondary market is structurally **buyer-supply mismatched** — rich seller pipeline, anemic local buyer base. This is a regulatory + capital-formation gap, not a deal-quality gap
- Vehicle structuring requires US/UK securities-law expertise (hence Dentons + NY banking co-founders)

### Recommendations & Insights

- **Secondaries are now THE liquidity vehicle for LatAm VC** — primary M&A and IPOs are too rare to satisfy DPI demand from Fund 1 / Fund 2 LPs
- The **emerging-markets globalization wave is finally reaching LatAm VC** as an asset class — "globalization started 50 years ago; emerging markets' turn has arrived"
- Long-term ambition: democratize emerging-markets investing across VC → credit → infrastructure → energy

### Notable Quotes

> "El plan grande es lanzar este vehículo. Empieza con $500M, ojalá termina en $10B en un par de años."

> "Los compradores [grandes] están afuera. Si me quedo en LATAM, tengo $1B; si salgo, tengo $4B."

> "Esas transacciones de uno, dos, tres millones — yo te consigo comprador. Pero cuando necesitas treinta, veinticinco, no tengo ni la más mínima idea."
